mission.title: Install and Configure Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
mission.description: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment, management, and operations of Kubernetes clusters in Azure. It is ideal for users who want to leverage Kubernetes without the overhead of managing the underlying infrastructure. Key trade-offs include reliance on Azure's infrastructure and potential costs associated with resource usage.

steps.title: Install Azure CLI
steps.description:
The Azure CLI is required to interact with Azure services, including AKS.

```bash

curl -sL https://aka.ms/InstallAzureCLIDeb | sudo bash

```

> Install Azure CLI on Debian-based systems.

steps.title: Create AKS Cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new AKS cluster with the specified Kubernetes version, node count, and region.

```bash

az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus

```

> Replace 'myResourceGroup' and 'myAKSCluster' with your desired names.
commands: az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus

steps.title: Configure kubectl
steps.description:
This command configures kubectl to use the newly created AKS cluster.

```bash

az aks get-credentials --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster

```

> This merges the AKS cluster credentials into your kubeconfig.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in the AKS cluster to ensure they are running.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see the nodes in 'Ready' status.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
Set up monitoring and autoscaling for your AKS cluster.

```bash

az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

az aks update --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --enable-cluster-autoscaler --min-count 1 --max-count 5

```

> Enables monitoring for the AKS cluster.
> Enables cluster autoscaler.
commands: az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

uninstall.title: Delete AKS Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the AKS cluster and all associated resources, which may lead to data loss.

```bash

az aks delete --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --yes --no-wait

```

> This will remove the cluster and associated resources.

uninstall.title: Remove Resource Group
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the resource group, which will also delete all resources within it.

```bash

az group delete --name myResourceGroup --yes --no-wait

```

> Be cautious as this will delete all resources in the resource group.

upgrade.title: Upgrade AKS Cluster
upgrade.description:
Backup your workloads, upgrade the AKS cluster, and verify the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o wide > backup_pods.txt

az aks upgrade --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --kubernetes-version 1.31

kubectl get nodes

```

> Backup current pod states.
> Upgrades the AKS cluster to the specified version.
> Verify that all nodes are in 'Ready' status after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create AKS cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Insufficient permissions or quota in the Azure subscription.

**Fix:**
Check your Azure subscription permissions and ensure you have enough quota for the resources.

troubleshooting.title: kubectl get nodes returns 'No resources found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** kubectl is not configured to point to the correct cluster.

**Fix:**
Run 'az aks get-credentials --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster' to configure kubectl.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources available'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The requested node count exceeds the available resources in the selected region.

**Fix:**
Reduce the node count or select a different region with available resources.

troubleshooting.title: Pods are stuck in 'Pending' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Insufficient resources or misconfigured node pools.

**Fix:**
Check the resource requests of the pods and ensure the node pool has enough capacity.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Azure CNI networking, improved integration with Azure Monitor.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for Kubernetes versions below 1.28.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 1.30, ensure your workloads are compatible with the new networking features.

resolution.summary: You should see a list of nodes with 'Ready' status when running 'kubectl get nodes'.
codeSnippets: az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus
az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the Azure CLI and kubectl installed and configured.
